modules.name: Week 1 — Intro, Observing the Sky, Orbits & Gravity
Week 3 — Solar System, The Moon, Origin (Ch 8-13 = references)
Week 4 — The Sun (Garden-Variety Star + Nuclear Powerhouse)
Week 5 — Analyzing Starlight, The Stars, Celestial Distances
Week 6 — Birth, Life & Death of Stars, Black Holes

glossary.def: A continuous spectrum crossed by dark lines, produced when light passes through a cooler thin gas that removes specific wavelengths.
A technology that rapidly deforms a telescope mirror to compensate for atmospheric turbulence and sharpen images from the ground.
The diameter of a telescope's main light-collecting lens or mirror; the single most important factor in its performance.
The point in an orbit farthest from the Sun.
A number describing how bright a star appears from Earth; smaller (and negative) numbers mean brighter objects.
The average distance between Earth and the Sun, about 150 million km; used to measure distances within the solar system.
The scientific study of objects beyond Earth and the processes by which they interact, plus the effort to organize that knowledge into a coherent history of the universe.
The blurring and twinkling of images caused by turbulence in Earth's atmosphere, which limits the resolution of ground-based telescopes.
The 23.5 deg angle between Earth's rotation axis and the perpendicular to its orbital plane; the cause of the seasons.
The event roughly 13.8 billion years ago from which the observable universe expanded; the beginning of space, time, matter, and energy as we know them.
The continuous spectrum emitted by an idealized object that absorbs and re-emits all radiation; its shape depends only on temperature.
An electronic detector that records light efficiently as digital images, having largely replaced photographic plates in astronomy.
The great circle on the celestial sphere directly above Earth's equator.
Either of the two points where Earth's rotation axis, extended into space, meets the celestial sphere; the sky appears to turn about these points.
An imaginary sphere surrounding Earth onto which the stars appear to be projected; a convenient model for describing positions in the sky.
A defect of lenses in which different colors focus at slightly different points, blurring the image; mirrors avoid this problem.
A region of the sky (and the recognizable star pattern within it); modern astronomy uses 88 official constellations covering the whole sky.
A spectrum showing light at all wavelengths with no gaps, produced by a hot, dense object such as a glowing solid or dense gas.
The branch of astronomy concerned with the origin, large-scale structure, and evolution of the universe as a whole.
The change in observed wavelength of a wave due to relative motion between source and observer; light is redshifted when the source recedes and blueshifted when it approaches.
A number from 0 to 1 describing how elongated an ellipse is; 0 is a perfect circle, values nearer 1 are more stretched.
The apparent annual path of the Sun against the background stars; also the plane of Earth's orbit.
Energy that travels through space as oscillating electric and magnetic fields, including radio, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays.
The full range of electromagnetic radiation arranged by wavelength or frequency, from radio waves to gamma rays.
A closed oval curve for which the sum of the distances from any point on it to two fixed points (foci) is constant.
A spectrum of bright lines at specific wavelengths, produced by a hot, thin (low-density) gas.
A small circle, used in the Ptolemaic geocentric model, on which a planet moved while the circle's center moved along a larger circle, to reproduce retrograde motion.
The two times each year when the Sun crosses the celestial equator and day and night are nearly equal everywhere.
The minimum speed an object needs to permanently leave the gravitational pull of a body without further propulsion.
The distance from a lens or mirror to the point where it brings parallel light to a focus.
One of two fixed points that define an ellipse; the Sun sits at one focus of a planet's orbit.
The number of wave crests passing a point each second; higher frequency means higher energy and shorter wavelength.
A vast, gravitationally bound system of stars, gas, dust, and dark matter, typically containing millions to trillions of stars.
An Earth-centered model of the cosmos in which the Sun, Moon, and planets orbit a stationary Earth.
A Sun-centered model in which Earth and the other planets orbit the Sun, proposed by Copernicus.
The circle where the sky appears to meet the ground; it divides the visible sky from the part below.
A tentative, testable explanation proposed to account for an observation.
An array of telescopes linked so their signals combine, achieving the resolution of a single instrument as large as their separation.
Each planet orbits the Sun in an ellipse, with the Sun at one focus of that ellipse.
A line joining a planet to the Sun sweeps out equal areas in equal times, so a planet moves faster when nearer the Sun.
The square of a planet's orbital period is proportional to the cube of its average distance from the Sun (P^2 = a^3 in years and AU).
Angular distance north or south of Earth's equator, measured in degrees from 0 deg at the equator to 90 deg at the poles.
A concise statement (often mathematical) describing a regularity in nature that holds under specified conditions.
The delay between when light leaves an object and when we observe it; because of it, looking far away means looking into the past.
A telescope's ability to collect light, which increases with the area of the aperture (proportional to the diameter squared).
The distance light travels in one year (about 9.5 trillion km); a unit of distance, not time.
Angular distance east or west of the prime meridian, measured in degrees.
An event in which Earth passes between the Sun and Moon, casting Earth's shadow on the Moon.
The spiral galaxy that contains our Sun and solar system, made up of a few hundred billion stars.
An object stays at rest or moves in a straight line at constant speed unless acted on by a net external force (inertia).
The acceleration of an object equals the net force on it divided by its mass (F = ma).
For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction; forces always come in pairs.
The speed an object must have to stay in a stable orbit, balancing its inertia against gravity.
The point in an orbit closest to the Sun.
The changing apparent shape of the sunlit Moon as seen from Earth, caused by the Moon's changing position relative to Earth and Sun.
A particle (quantum) of electromagnetic energy; light behaves as both a wave and a stream of photons.
The slow (~26,000-year) conical wobble of Earth's rotation axis, which gradually changes which star marks the celestial pole.
A telescope, usually a large dish, designed to collect and focus radio waves from space.
A telescope that uses a curved mirror to collect and focus light; nearly all large research telescopes are reflectors.
A telescope that uses a lens to bend (refract) and focus incoming light.
The ability of a telescope to show fine detail and separate objects that are close together in the sky.
The temporary apparent backward (east-to-west) drift of a planet against the stars, naturally explained by the heliocentric model.
The orbital motion of one body around another; Earth's revolution around the Sun takes one year.
The spin of a body about its own axis; Earth's rotation produces day and night.
The process of forming a hypothesis, testing it against observation or experiment, and revising ideas based on the evidence.
A way of writing very large or very small numbers as a coefficient times a power of ten (e.g., 3 x 10^8).
A division of the year caused primarily by the tilt of Earth's axis, which changes the directness of sunlight and length of daylight.
Half the longest diameter of an ellipse; for a planet's orbit it equals the average distance from the Sun.
The rotation period of Earth measured relative to the stars, about 23 hours 56 minutes.
The time from one local noon to the next, averaging 24 hours; slightly longer than the sidereal day because Earth also moves along its orbit.
An event in which the Moon passes between the Sun and Earth, blocking the Sun's light for observers in the shadow.
The Sun together with everything gravitationally bound to it: planets, moons, asteroids, comets, and dust.
The two times each year when the Sun reaches its greatest distance north or south of the celestial equator, giving the longest and shortest days.
A telescope placed above Earth's atmosphere to avoid blurring and to observe wavelengths the atmosphere blocks (such as UV, X-ray, and much infrared).
The technique of spreading light into its spectrum to analyze the composition, temperature, motion, and other properties of a source.
The constant speed at which all electromagnetic radiation travels in a vacuum, about 300,000 km/s.
The rule that the total energy radiated per unit area by a hot object rises steeply with temperature (as the fourth power).
Rotation in which a body spins once per orbit, so it always shows the same face to its partner; the Moon does this with Earth.
An instrument that collects and focuses electromagnetic radiation to make faint, distant objects easier to detect and study.
A well-tested, broad explanation supported by a large body of evidence; in science, much stronger than an everyday 'guess.'
Newton's law that every mass attracts every other mass with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
The totality of all matter, energy, space, and time; everything that exists.
The distance between successive crests of a wave; for light it determines color and where the radiation falls in the spectrum.
The rule that hotter objects radiate most strongly at shorter wavelengths, so peak color shifts toward the blue as temperature rises.
The point on the celestial sphere directly overhead an observer.
The band of constellations the Sun appears to pass through along the ecliptic over the course of a year.

flashcards.front: What is one light-year, and is it a unit of time or distance?
flashcards.back: A unit of distance: the distance light travels in one year, about 9.5 trillion km.

flashcards.front: How old is the universe?
flashcards.back: About 13.8 billion years (since the Big Bang).

flashcards.front: Roughly how many stars are in the Milky Way?
flashcards.back: A few hundred billion stars.

flashcards.front: Why does looking at distant objects mean looking back in time?
flashcards.back: Light travels at a finite speed, so we see objects as they were when their light left them.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a hypothesis and a theory in science?
flashcards.back: A hypothesis is a tentative testable idea; a theory is a broad, well-tested explanation backed by extensive evidence.

flashcards.front: What is the approximate distance from Earth to the Sun, and what is it called?
flashcards.back: About 150 million km, called one astronomical unit (AU).

flashcards.front: Order these from smallest to largest: galaxy, solar system, star, universe.
flashcards.back: Star, solar system, galaxy, universe.

flashcards.front: Why do astronomers use scientific notation?
flashcards.back: To write extremely large and small numbers compactly and avoid errors with many zeros.

flashcards.front: What does cosmology study?
flashcards.back: The origin, large-scale structure, and evolution of the universe as a whole.

flashcards.front: What is the ecliptic?
flashcards.back: The Sun's apparent yearly path against the background stars, which is also the plane of Earth's orbit.

flashcards.front: What causes the apparent daily rotation of the sky?
flashcards.back: Earth's rotation on its axis from west to east.

flashcards.front: What is retrograde motion and what causes it?
flashcards.back: The apparent backward drift of a planet against the stars, caused by Earth overtaking (or being overtaken by) another planet as both orbit the Sun.

flashcards.front: Who proposed a Sun-centered (heliocentric) model in the 1500s?
flashcards.back: Nicolaus Copernicus.

flashcards.front: How did the geocentric model explain retrograde motion?
flashcards.back: With epicycles: planets moved on small circles whose centers moved along larger circles around Earth.

flashcards.front: What is precession?
flashcards.back: A slow ~26,000-year wobble of Earth's axis that changes which star is the pole star over time.

flashcards.front: In the magnitude system, does a larger or smaller number mean a brighter star?
flashcards.back: A smaller (or more negative) number means a brighter star.

flashcards.front: How many official constellations are there?
flashcards.back: 88, covering the entire sky.

flashcards.front: What is the zenith?
flashcards.back: The point in the sky directly overhead the observer.

flashcards.front: State Kepler's first law.
flashcards.back: Planets orbit the Sun in ellipses with the Sun at one focus.

flashcards.front: State Kepler's second law and its consequence.
flashcards.back: A planet-Sun line sweeps equal areas in equal times, so planets move faster near the Sun (perihelion) and slower when far (aphelion).

flashcards.front: State Kepler's third law as a formula.
flashcards.back: P^2 = a^3, where P is the orbital period in years and a is the semimajor axis in AU.

flashcards.front: What does eccentricity measure?
flashcards.back: How stretched-out an ellipse is: 0 = circle, approaching 1 = very elongated.

flashcards.front: What is Newton's second law?
flashcards.back: Force equals mass times acceleration (F = ma).

flashcards.front: What does Newton's law of universal gravitation say about distance?
flashcards.back: Gravitational force decreases with the square of the distance (inverse-square law).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between perihelion and aphelion?
flashcards.back: Perihelion is the closest point to the Sun; aphelion is the farthest point.

flashcards.front: What is escape velocity?
flashcards.back: The minimum speed to permanently leave a body's gravity without more thrust.

flashcards.front: How did Newton explain Kepler's laws?
flashcards.back: He showed they follow mathematically from his laws of motion plus the inverse-square law of gravity.

flashcards.front: What causes Earth's seasons?
flashcards.back: The 23.5 deg tilt of Earth's axis, which changes how directly sunlight strikes a hemisphere and how long the Sun is up — not Earth's distance from the Sun.

flashcards.front: Why do we always see the same side of the Moon?
flashcards.back: The Moon is in synchronous rotation: it spins once per orbit, so the same face always points toward Earth.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a solstice and an equinox?
flashcards.back: Solstices are the longest/shortest days (Sun farthest from celestial equator); equinoxes have nearly equal day and night (Sun on the celestial equator).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between rotation and revolution?
flashcards.back: Rotation is spinning on an axis (day/night); revolution is orbiting another body (the year).

flashcards.front: During a solar eclipse, what lies between the Sun and Earth?
flashcards.back: The Moon (it blocks the Sun's light).

flashcards.front: During a lunar eclipse, what lies between the Sun and Moon?
flashcards.back: Earth (its shadow falls on the Moon).

flashcards.front: Why isn't there an eclipse every month?
flashcards.back: The Moon's orbit is tilted about 5 deg to Earth's orbit, so the three bodies usually don't line up exactly.

flashcards.front: Why is a solar day slightly longer than a sidereal day?
flashcards.back: Because while Earth rotates, it also moves along its orbit, so it must turn a bit extra (~4 minutes) to bring the Sun back to the same position.

flashcards.front: Does the Moon produce its own light?
flashcards.back: No; we see sunlight reflected off the Moon, and the phases depend on the Sun-Earth-Moon geometry.

flashcards.front: List the electromagnetic spectrum from longest to shortest wavelength.
flashcards.back: Radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma ray.

flashcards.front: How are a photon's energy, frequency, and wavelength related?
flashcards.back: Higher frequency = shorter wavelength = higher energy; longer wavelength = lower frequency = lower energy.

flashcards.front: What is the speed of light in a vacuum?
flashcards.back: About 300,000 km/s, the same for all electromagnetic radiation.

flashcards.front: What kind of source produces a continuous spectrum?
flashcards.back: A hot, dense object (a glowing solid, liquid, or dense gas).

flashcards.front: What produces an emission-line spectrum?
flashcards.back: A hot, thin (low-density) gas, which emits light only at specific wavelengths.

flashcards.front: What produces an absorption-line spectrum?
flashcards.back: A continuous source seen through a cooler thin gas, which removes specific wavelengths and leaves dark lines.

flashcards.front: What does Wien's law tell us about a hot vs. cool star?
flashcards.back: Hotter objects peak at shorter (bluer) wavelengths; cooler objects peak at longer (redder) wavelengths — so color indicates temperature.

flashcards.front: What does a redshift in a star's spectrum indicate?
flashcards.back: The source is moving away from us (Doppler effect); a blueshift means it is approaching.

flashcards.front: Why is spectroscopy so powerful in astronomy?
flashcards.back: Spectral lines reveal composition, temperature, density, and motion of objects we can never visit.

flashcards.front: What is the most important property of a telescope, and why?
flashcards.back: Its aperture (diameter): a larger aperture collects more light and can resolve finer detail.

flashcards.front: How does light-gathering power depend on aperture?
flashcards.back: It is proportional to the area, i.e., to the diameter squared — doubling the diameter collects four times as much light.

flashcards.front: Why are nearly all large modern telescopes reflectors rather than refractors?
flashcards.back: Mirrors avoid chromatic aberration, can be made very large and supported from behind, and large quality lenses are heavy and hard to make.

flashcards.front: What is chromatic aberration and which telescope type suffers from it?
flashcards.back: Different colors focusing at different points, blurring the image; it affects refractors (lenses), not reflectors (mirrors).

flashcards.front: What causes stars to twinkle, and how does it affect telescopes?
flashcards.back: Turbulence in Earth's atmosphere (atmospheric seeing), which blurs images and limits ground-based resolution.

flashcards.front: What does adaptive optics do?
flashcards.back: It rapidly reshapes a telescope mirror to cancel atmospheric distortion, sharpening ground-based images.

flashcards.front: Why put telescopes in space?
flashcards.back: To escape atmospheric blurring and to observe wavelengths (UV, X-ray, much IR) that the atmosphere absorbs.

flashcards.front: What is a CCD and what did it replace?
flashcards.back: A charge-coupled device, an efficient electronic light detector that replaced photographic plates.

flashcards.front: How does an interferometer improve resolution?
flashcards.back: By combining signals from telescopes spread apart, it achieves the resolving power of a single dish as large as their separation.

questions.text: A light-year is a measure of which quantity?
Approximately how old is the universe?
Because light travels at a finite speed, observing a distant galaxy shows it as it was in the past.
In science, the word 'theory' means little more than a casual guess.
The average distance between Earth and the Sun is called one astronomical ______.
The galaxy that contains our solar system is the ______ Way.
Explain why astronomers say that telescopes are, in effect, time machines.
questions.answer: Light takes time to reach us, so the farther away an object is, the longer ago its light left it; observing distant objects therefore shows them as they were in the past, not as they are now.

questions.text: Describe the basic steps of the scientific method.
questions.answer: Make observations, propose a testable hypothesis, make predictions, test them with further observation or experiment, and revise or discard the hypothesis based on the evidence; well-supported explanations may become theories.

questions.text: Match each scale or object to the correct description.
pairs: The Sun plus everything bound to it
Billions of stars bound by gravity
The origin event of the universe

questions.text: Which list is correctly ordered from smallest to largest in physical scale?
The Milky Way contains roughly a few hundred billion stars.
The apparent backward motion of a planet against the background stars is called:
Who is credited with reviving the heliocentric (Sun-centered) model in the 1500s?
The daily rising and setting of stars is caused by Earth's rotation on its axis.
In the magnitude system, brighter stars have larger positive numbers.
The Sun's apparent yearly path among the stars is called the ______.
Ptolemy's geocentric model used small circles called ______ to reproduce retrograde motion.
Explain how the heliocentric model accounts for the retrograde motion of planets like Mars.
questions.answer: As Earth, on a faster inner orbit, overtakes a slower outer planet like Mars, the outer planet appears to drift backward against the distant stars for a time before resuming its normal eastward motion; it is an effect of the changing line of sight, not real backward motion.

questions.text: Why did the geocentric model persist for so long despite being incorrect?
questions.answer: It matched everyday experience (Earth feels stationary), agreed with the authority of Aristotle and Ptolemy, fit religious/philosophical views of the time, and—with enough epicycles—could predict planetary positions reasonably well.

questions.text: Match each term to its description.
pairs: Sun's yearly path among the stars
Point the sky appears to rotate around

questions.text: Earth's slow ~26,000-year axial wobble is called:
There are 88 official modern constellations covering the whole sky.
According to Kepler's first law, the orbit of a planet is:
questions.answer: An ellipse with the Sun at one focus
options: An ellipse with the Sun at one focus

questions.text: Kepler's third law is best written as:
A planet moves fastest in its orbit when it is at perihelion (closest to the Sun).
Gravitational force is directly proportional to the distance between two objects.
The point in an orbit closest to the Sun is called ______.
Newton's second law of motion is written as the equation F = m______.
Using Kepler's third law, explain how you would find the orbital period of a planet whose semimajor axis is 4 AU.
questions.answer: Kepler's third law gives P^2 = a^3. With a = 4 AU, a^3 = 64, so P^2 = 64 and P = 8 years.

questions.text: Describe how gravity keeps a planet in orbit instead of flying off in a straight line.
questions.answer: By inertia (Newton's first law) a planet tends to move in a straight line, but the Sun's gravity continuously pulls it toward the Sun. The constant sideways pull bends the straight-line path into a closed, curved orbit; the planet is essentially 'falling' around the Sun.

questions.text: Match each law to its statement.
pairs: Orbits are ellipses with the Sun at a focus
Equal areas swept in equal times

questions.text: The inverse-square nature of gravity means that if the distance between two masses doubles, the gravitational force becomes:
Newton showed that Kepler's three laws follow from his laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation.
Earth's seasons are caused primarily by:
questions.answer: The tilt of Earth's rotation axis
options: Earth's changing distance from the Sun
The tilt of Earth's rotation axis

questions.text: We always see the same side of the Moon because the Moon:
options: Rotates much faster than it orbits
Is tidally pushed away from Earth

questions.text: During a total solar eclipse, the Moon is positioned between the Sun and Earth.
Earth's seasons occur because Earth is much closer to the Sun in summer.
The two days each year when day and night are nearly equal in length are called the ______.
Earth's rotation axis is tilted about 23.5 ______ from the perpendicular to its orbit.
Explain why the tilt of Earth's axis, not its distance from the Sun, drives the seasons.
questions.answer: The tilt means each hemisphere is angled toward the Sun for part of the year (more direct sunlight and longer days = summer) and away for another part (less direct light, shorter days = winter). When one hemisphere has summer the other has winter, which couldn't happen if distance were the cause; in fact Earth is slightly closer to the Sun during Northern Hemisphere winter.

questions.text: Why don't solar and lunar eclipses happen every month?
questions.answer: The Moon's orbit is tilted about 5 degrees relative to Earth's orbit around the Sun, so most months the Moon passes above or below the exact Sun-Earth line. Eclipses only occur when the alignment is close enough during new moon (solar) or full moon (lunar).

questions.text: Match each event to its Sun-Earth-Moon geometry.
pairs: Moon opposite the Sun, fully lit face toward us
Moon between us and the Sun, dark face toward us

questions.text: A sidereal day (relative to the stars) is about:
Rotation produces day and night, while revolution around the Sun produces the year.
Which of the following has the longest wavelength?
A hot, low-density gas produces which kind of spectrum?
All forms of electromagnetic radiation travel at the same speed in a vacuum.
A blueshift in a spectrum means the object is moving away from us.
The change in observed wavelength caused by a source's motion toward or away from us is called the ______ effect.
According to Wien's law, the ______ an object is, the shorter the wavelength at which it radiates most strongly.
Explain how astronomers can determine a star's temperature from its light.
questions.answer: A star radiates approximately as a blackbody, so the wavelength at which it is brightest depends on its temperature (Wien's law). Measuring the peak (or overall color) reveals the temperature: bluer peaks mean hotter stars, redder peaks mean cooler ones.

questions.text: Describe how the three types of spectra (continuous, emission, absorption) are produced.
questions.answer: A hot dense object gives a continuous spectrum (all wavelengths). A hot thin gas gives an emission-line spectrum (bright lines at specific wavelengths). A continuous source viewed through a cooler thin gas gives an absorption-line spectrum (dark lines where the gas removes specific wavelengths). These are summarized by Kirchhoff's laws.

questions.text: Match each spectrum or law to its description.
pairs: Produced by a hot, dense object
Produced by a hot, thin gas
Dark lines from a cool gas in front of a hot source
Hotter objects peak at shorter wavelengths

questions.text: If a star's spectral lines are shifted toward longer (redder) wavelengths, the star is:
An emission-line spectrum can reveal which chemical elements are present in a glowing gas.
The single most important property of a telescope is its:
If you double the diameter of a telescope's mirror, its light-gathering power increases by a factor of:
Reflecting telescopes use a curved mirror to gather and focus light.
Refracting telescopes are free of chromatic aberration.
The blurring of images caused by turbulence in Earth's atmosphere is called atmospheric ______.
An electronic detector called a ______ has replaced photographic plates for recording astronomical images.
Explain two distinct advantages of putting a telescope in space rather than on the ground.
questions.answer: First, it escapes atmospheric turbulence (seeing), allowing much sharper images. Second, it can observe wavelengths the atmosphere absorbs—such as ultraviolet, X-rays, and much of the infrared—that never reach the ground.

questions.text: Why do astronomers prefer building larger telescopes, and what two benefits does a larger aperture provide?
questions.answer: A larger aperture collects more light (light-gathering power scales with diameter squared), letting astronomers see fainter objects, and it provides finer resolution, letting them distinguish more detail and separate closely spaced objects.

questions.text: Match each instrument or technique to its purpose.
pairs: Uses a mirror to focus light

questions.text: Chromatic aberration is a problem associated specifically with:
Radio telescopes must generally be much larger than visible-light telescopes to achieve comparable resolution.
Define "Astronomy" and "Astrology."
questions.answer: Astronomy is the scientific study of celestial objects (stars, planets, galaxies, and so on), the universe as a whole, and the physical processes that govern them, based on observation and evidence. Astrology is the non-scientific belief that the positions of the Sun, Moon, and planets influence human affairs and personalities; it is not a science, makes no testable, reliably confirmed predictions, and should not be confused with astronomy.

questions.text: Determine the shape and size of Earth.
questions.answer: Earth is very nearly a sphere (slightly flattened at the poles). Its shape is shown by ships disappearing hull-first over the horizon, Earth's round shadow on the Moon during lunar eclipses, and the way visible constellations change with latitude. Its size was first estimated by Eratosthenes, who compared the Sun's noon angle at two cities a known distance apart and computed a circumference close to the true value of about 40,000 km (radius about 6,371 km).

questions.text: Discuss how the Scientific Method is used to solve problems.
questions.answer: The scientific method solves problems by insisting on testable, evidence-based explanations. One observes a phenomenon, forms a hypothesis, predicts what should follow if the hypothesis is true, tests it through observation or experiment, and then confirms, refines, or rejects it. Repeating this cycle weeds out wrong ideas, and explanations that survive extensive testing become accepted theories.

questions.text: Discuss the concept of "distances" and the importance of units.
questions.answer: Astronomical distances span an enormous range, so choosing the right unit is essential to keep numbers manageable and meaningful. Within the solar system, distances are given in astronomical units (AU, the Earth-Sun distance); between stars and galaxies, in light-years or parsecs. Besides convenience, these units convey scale and, for light-years, the look-back time, since light from a distant object left it long ago.

questions.text: Define the main features of the celestial sphere.
questions.answer: The celestial sphere is an imaginary sphere surrounding Earth onto which the stars appear projected. Its key features are the celestial poles (above Earth's poles, the points the sky seems to rotate around), the celestial equator (above Earth's equator), the ecliptic (the Sun's yearly path, tilted 23.5 degrees to the equator), the zenith (the point overhead), the horizon, and the meridian (the north-south line through the zenith).

questions.text: Describe how the motions of the Sun, Moon, stars, and planets appear to us on Earth.
questions.answer: All sky objects rise in the east and set in the west each day because of Earth's rotation. Over a year the Sun drifts eastward through the zodiac along the ecliptic (from Earth's orbit), while the Moon moves eastward and cycles through its phases about once a month. The planets generally drift eastward against the stars but occasionally appear to reverse into retrograde motion.

questions.text: Describe Ptolemy's geocentric system of planetary motion.
questions.answer: In Ptolemy's geocentric system, a stationary Earth sits at the center while the Sun, Moon, planets, and stars revolve around it. To reproduce the observed motions, including retrograde loops and varying speeds, Ptolemy had each planet move on a small circle (epicycle) whose center moved along a larger circle (deferent), with an offset reference point (the equant) added to improve the fit.

questions.text: Explain how Copernicus developed the heliocentric model of the solar system.
questions.answer: Dissatisfied with the complexity of the Ptolemaic system, Copernicus proposed placing the Sun, not Earth, at the center, with Earth and the other planets orbiting it and Earth also rotating daily. This Sun-centered arrangement naturally explained retrograde motion (as Earth overtakes outer planets) and gave a sensible ordering and spacing of the planets, offering a simpler overall picture.

questions.text: Describe Galileo's discoveries concerning the study of motion and forces.
questions.answer: Galileo established key ideas about motion: that objects of different mass fall at the same rate, and that a moving object continues moving unless a force acts on it (the principle of inertia), overturning the old belief that motion needs a constant push. His telescopic discoveries, the moons of Jupiter, the phases of Venus, lunar craters, and sunspots, provided evidence for the heliocentric model and laid groundwork that Newton later built upon.

questions.text: Describe how Tycho Brahe and Johannes Kepler contributed to our understanding of how planets move around the Sun.
questions.answer: Tycho Brahe made decades of remarkably precise naked-eye measurements of planetary positions, the best of the pre-telescope era. Kepler inherited that data and analyzed it, especially for Mars, discovering that the orbits are ellipses, not circles, and formulating his three laws of planetary motion. Brahe supplied the precision and Kepler supplied the mathematical insight.

questions.text: Explain Kepler's three laws of planetary motion.
questions.answer: First law: each planet orbits the Sun in an ellipse with the Sun at one focus. Second law: a line from the Sun to a planet sweeps out equal areas in equal times, so a planet moves faster when nearer the Sun. Third law: the square of a planet's orbital period equals the cube of its semimajor axis (P^2 = a^3, with P in years and a in AU).

questions.text: Describe Newton's three laws of motion.
questions.answer: First law: an object stays at rest or in uniform straight-line motion unless acted on by a net force (inertia). Second law: a net force produces an acceleration equal to the force divided by the mass (F = ma). Third law: for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction, so forces always occur in pairs.

questions.text: Describe how Newton's universal law of gravitation extends our understanding of Kepler's laws.
questions.answer: Newton's law of gravitation states that every mass attracts every other with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. By combining this with his laws of motion, Newton showed that Kepler's three laws are not just empirical patterns but necessary consequences of gravity, and he generalized them, explaining why orbits are conic sections and adding the masses of the bodies into a more complete form of the third law.

questions.text: Compare the orbital characteristics of the planets in the solar system.
questions.answer: The planets all orbit the Sun in the same direction, in nearly the same plane, and in roughly circular orbits. Inner planets are closer, move faster, and have shorter periods, while outer planets are farther, move slower, and have longer periods (Neptune has the largest orbit and longest period). Mercury has the most eccentric and most inclined orbit of the eight, while most planets follow nearly circular paths.

questions.text: Explain how an object (such as a satellite) can be put into orbit around Earth.
questions.answer: A satellite stays in orbit by moving sideways fast enough that, as gravity pulls it down, its falling path curves around Earth at the same rate the surface curves away, so it continually 'falls around' the planet without hitting it. Reaching this requires accelerating it to orbital velocity (about 8 km/s for low Earth orbit) at sufficient altitude, which is what launch rockets provide.

questions.text: Explain how an object (such as a planetary probe) can escape from orbit.
questions.answer: To leave Earth permanently, a probe must reach escape velocity (about 11 km/s from Earth's surface), the speed at which its kinetic energy exceeds the energy binding it to Earth's gravity. At or above that speed it can coast away without falling back, allowing it to travel to other planets.

questions.text: Explain how the planet Neptune was discovered.
questions.answer: Astronomers noticed that Uranus was not following its predicted orbit, suggesting an unseen body was tugging on it. Using Newton's law of gravitation, Le Verrier and Adams independently calculated where such a planet should be, and in 1846 Neptune was found very close to the predicted position, which is why it is called the planet discovered 'with mathematics.'

notes.title: Ch. 6 — Astronomical Instruments
notes.body:
# Astronomical Instruments

## What telescopes do
- Collect and focus electromagnetic radiation so faint, distant objects can be studied.
- They do NOT just magnify — their main job is **gathering light** and **resolving detail**.

## Two basic optical designs
- **Refractor:** uses a **lens**. Suffers **chromatic aberration** (colors focus differently); hard to make large.
- **Reflector:** uses a **mirror**. No chromatic aberration; can be made huge. **All large research telescopes are reflectors.**

## Aperture = the key spec
- **Aperture** = diameter of the main lens/mirror.
- **Light-gathering power ∝ diameter²** → double the diameter = **4×** the light.
- Bigger aperture also = **better resolution** (finer detail).

## Detectors
- **CCDs** (electronic) replaced photographic plates — far more efficient and digital.

## Earth's atmosphere = the enemy
- **Atmospheric seeing:** turbulence blurs/twinkles images, limiting ground resolution.
- **Adaptive optics:** mirror flexes rapidly to cancel turbulence → sharp ground images.

## Beyond the ground / beyond visible light
- **Space telescopes:** avoid seeing AND reach blocked wavelengths (UV, X-ray, much IR).
- **Radio telescopes:** big dishes for radio waves (must be large for good resolution).
- **Interferometers:** link separated telescopes → resolution of a dish as wide as their spacing.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ **Aperture** is the most important property (light-gathering + resolution).
- ★ Light-gathering ∝ **diameter²** (2× diameter → 4× light).
- ★ Big telescopes = **reflectors** (mirrors); refractors suffer **chromatic aberration**.
- ★ Space telescopes: escape **seeing** + reach **UV/X-ray/IR** blocked by the atmosphere.
- ★ Interferometry links dishes for the resolution of a much larger instrument.

notes.title: Ch. 5 — Radiation and Spectra
notes.body:
# Radiation and Spectra

## Light = electromagnetic radiation
- Oscillating electric + magnetic fields; travels at **~300,000 km/s** in vacuum.
- Wave AND particle: a particle of light is a **photon**.
- **Wavelength** ↔ **frequency** ↔ **energy**:
  - short wavelength = high frequency = high energy
  - long wavelength = low frequency = low energy

## The electromagnetic spectrum (long → short λ)
- **Radio → microwave → infrared → visible → ultraviolet → X-ray → gamma ray**
- Visible light is a tiny slice in the middle.

## Blackbody / thermal radiation
- A hot dense object radiates a continuous spectrum set only by its **temperature**.
- **Wien's law:** hotter → peaks at **shorter (bluer)** wavelength → color tells temperature.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann law:** total energy radiated rises as **T⁴** (very steep).

## Three spectra (Kirchhoff's laws)
- **Continuous:** hot, **dense** object (solid/dense gas).
- **Emission lines:** hot, **thin** gas → bright lines at specific λ.
- **Absorption lines:** continuous source seen **through** a cooler thin gas → dark lines.
- Lines act like fingerprints → identify **elements**.

## Doppler effect
- Source receding → **redshift** (longer λ).
- Source approaching → **blueshift** (shorter λ).
- Lets us measure motion of stars and galaxies along the line of sight.

## Why it matters
- **Spectroscopy** reveals composition, temperature, density, and motion of objects we can never visit.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ EM spectrum order: radio → micro → IR → visible → UV → X-ray → gamma.
- ★ Shorter λ = higher frequency = higher energy.
- ★ Wien's law: hotter = bluer peak (color → temperature).
- ★ Spectra: dense=continuous, thin hot gas=emission, cool gas in front=absorption.
- ★ Doppler: redshift = receding, blueshift = approaching.

notes.title: Ch. 4 — Earth, Moon, and Sky
notes.body:
# Earth, Moon, and Sky

## Position on Earth
- **Latitude:** degrees N/S of the equator (0–90 deg).
- **Longitude:** degrees E/W of the prime meridian.

## Rotation vs. revolution
- **Rotation:** spin on axis → day/night.
- **Revolution:** orbit the Sun → the year.
- **Sidereal day:** ~23h 56m (vs. stars). **Solar day:** ~24h (vs. Sun) — longer because Earth also moves along its orbit.

## Seasons (cause = AXIAL TILT, not distance)
- Axis tilted **23.5 deg**.
- Hemisphere tilted toward Sun → direct light + long days = **summer**.
- Opposite hemisphere has winter at the same time → distance can't be the cause.
- **Solstices:** longest/shortest days (Sun farthest from celestial equator).
- **Equinoxes:** ~equal day/night (Sun on celestial equator).

## The Moon
- Shines by **reflected sunlight**; phases come from Sun-Earth-Moon geometry.
- **Synchronous rotation:** spins once per orbit → same face always toward Earth.
- New moon (between us & Sun) → full moon (opposite Sun) and back, ~29.5 days.

## Eclipses
- **Solar:** Moon between Sun and Earth (at new moon).
- **Lunar:** Earth between Sun and Moon (at full moon).
- Not monthly because the Moon's orbit is tilted ~5 deg → alignment is usually off.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Seasons = **axial tilt (23.5 deg)**, NOT Earth–Sun distance.
- ★ Same face of Moon → **synchronous rotation** (spins once per orbit).
- ★ Solar eclipse: **Moon in the middle**; lunar eclipse: **Earth in the middle**.
- ★ No monthly eclipses because the Moon's orbit is tilted ~5 deg.
- ★ Solstice = longest/shortest day; equinox = equal day/night.

notes.title: Ch. 3 — Orbits and Gravity
notes.body:
# Orbits and Gravity

## Kepler's three laws (empirical, from Tycho's data)
- **1st (Law of Ellipses):** Planets orbit the Sun in **ellipses**, Sun at one **focus**.
- **2nd (Law of Equal Areas):** The planet-Sun line sweeps **equal areas in equal times** → planet is **fastest at perihelion**, slowest at aphelion.
- **3rd (Harmonic Law):** **P^2 = a^3** (P in years, a in AU). Bigger orbit → much longer period.

## Ellipse vocabulary
- **Foci:** two defining points; Sun sits at one.
- **Eccentricity (e):** 0 = circle, near 1 = very stretched.
- **Semimajor axis (a):** half the long axis = average distance from Sun.
- **Perihelion:** closest to Sun. **Aphelion:** farthest.

## Newton's laws of motion
- **1st:** inertia — no net force → no change in motion.
- **2nd:** **F = ma**.
- **3rd:** equal and opposite reaction; forces come in pairs.

## Universal gravitation
- Every mass attracts every other mass.
- Force ∝ (m1 × m2) and ∝ 1/distance² → **inverse-square law**.
- Double the distance → force drops to **1/4**.

## Why orbits happen
- Inertia wants straight-line motion; gravity pulls toward the central body.
- The combination = continuous 'falling around' → a closed orbit.
- **Escape velocity:** minimum speed to leave for good.

## Newton unifies it
- Kepler's laws are *derived* from Newton's motion laws + inverse-square gravity.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Memorize **P^2 = a^3** (years, AU). a=4 → P=8 yr.
- ★ Fastest at **perihelion** (2nd law / equal areas).
- ★ Gravity is **inverse-square**: 2× distance → 1/4 force.
- ★ Orbits = inertia + gravity (falling around the Sun).
- ★ Newton's 2nd law: **F = ma**.

notes.title: Ch. 2 — Observing the Sky: The Birth of Astronomy
notes.body:
# Observing the Sky: The Birth of Astronomy

## The celestial sphere (a model)
- An imaginary sphere with the stars 'attached'; useful for describing positions.
- **Celestial poles:** Earth's axis extended; sky appears to spin around them.
- **Celestial equator:** projection of Earth's equator.
- **Zenith:** point directly overhead. **Horizon:** where sky meets ground.

## Apparent motions
- **Daily:** sky rises in east, sets in west — caused by Earth's **rotation**.
- **Yearly:** Sun drifts eastward through the **zodiac** along the **ecliptic** — caused by Earth's **orbit**.
- The ecliptic is also the plane of Earth's orbit.

## Brightness: the magnitude system
- Smaller number = brighter; the brightest objects have negative magnitudes.
- It is a backwards/counterintuitive scale (inherited from the Greeks).

## Ancient models
- **Geocentric (Earth-centered):** Aristotle/Ptolemy. Used **epicycles** to fake retrograde motion.
- **Heliocentric (Sun-centered):** Copernicus (1500s). Retrograde motion falls out naturally from Earth overtaking outer planets.

## Retrograde motion
- A planet appears to drift backward (westward) for weeks, then resumes eastward motion.
- Heliocentric explanation: changing line of sight as Earth laps an outer planet — no real reversal.

## Precession
- Slow ~26,000-year conical wobble of Earth's axis.
- Changes which star is the 'pole star' over millennia (e.g., not always Polaris).

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ Daily sky motion = Earth's **rotation**; yearly Sun motion = Earth's **orbit**.
- ★ Ecliptic = Sun's apparent yearly path = plane of Earth's orbit.
- ★ Geocentric used **epicycles**; heliocentric (Copernicus) explains retrograde naturally.
- ★ Magnitudes: **smaller/negative = brighter**.
- ★ Precession ≈ 26,000-year axial wobble; changes the pole star.

notes.title: Ch. 1 — Science and the Universe: A Brief Tour
notes.body:
# Science and the Universe: A Brief Tour

## What astronomy is
- Astronomy is the study of everything beyond Earth and how those objects interact.
- It is also the effort to assemble what we learn into a single history of the universe, from the Big Bang to now.
- Science is a *progress report*: ideas change as new instruments and techniques let us probe deeper.

## A sense of scale (key distances)
- **Astronomical unit (AU):** Earth–Sun distance, ~150 million km. Used inside the solar system.
- **Light-year:** distance light travels in one year, ~9.5 trillion km. Used between stars and galaxies. (It is a *distance*, not a time.)
- Light speed is finite, so distance = looking back in time.

## Structure of the universe (small to large)
- Stars (like the Sun)
- Solar system (Sun + planets, moons, asteroids, comets)
- Galaxy (the Milky Way: a few hundred billion stars)
- Groups/clusters of galaxies
- The universe as a whole

## Time
- The universe is about **13.8 billion years** old (since the Big Bang).
- Earth and the solar system are about **4.6 billion years** old.

## How science works
- **Hypothesis:** a testable tentative explanation.
- **Theory:** a broad, repeatedly tested explanation backed by lots of evidence (a *strong* word in science).
- **Law of nature:** a concise, often mathematical statement of a regularity.

## ★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- ★ A light-year is a unit of **distance**, not time.
- ★ One AU ≈ 150 million km (Earth–Sun distance).
- ★ Universe age ≈ 13.8 billion years; solar system/Earth ≈ 4.6 billion years.
- ★ Finite light speed → distant = past ("telescopes are time machines").
- ★ In science, a "theory" is well-supported, not a mere guess.
